strands.title: Research Question REFRAMED — Separating CPU from Cache Management (CRITICAL PIVOT)
strands.content: Cycles 12-16 surgically separated H0 (cognitive processor viability) from H1 (memory management protocol). H0 tests whether a transformer can function as a BOUNDED REASONING KERNEL—a CPU that executes structured computation, makes explicit state references, and halts when done—NOT about memory management. Pichay's memory control language is a SECONDARY LAYER, a protocol between CPU and memory subsystem. These are DISTINCT HYPOTHESES with different success criteria. Cycle 19 now introduces a CRITICAL ECONOMIC DIMENSION: the cost structure of API-based transformers (prefix caching at 10% cost for cache hits, variable output token pricing with recency discounts, context stability as first-class concern) directly constrains pipeline design. The architecture remains valid (TRANSFORMER = ALU, MEMORY = hierarchy, CONTROLLER = orchestration), but the controller loop now has a DUAL MANDATE: (1) FUNCTIONAL: execute bounded reasoning correctly, (2) ECONOMIC: minimize token costs by maintaining prefix cache stability, exploiting output recency discounts, and choosing between descriptive (model-decides) vs. prescriptive (Haiku triage) pipelines. The economic pressure may OVERRIDE the intellectually cleaner descriptive approach—the manifest-and-fault pattern requires two-shot per step (manifest read + full context re-read), multiplying Opus token costs, whereas Haiku prefetching (one cheap triage, one expensive reasoning pass) eliminates round trips and is 'an order of magnitude' cheaper. The H0 baseline (naive memory subsystem, no optimization) must now be evaluated not just on processor properties (schema adherence, explicit halts, resource-groundedness, task competence) but also on COST-PER-TASK. The 'garbage heap' from cycles 12-15 is now understood to have had a THIRD hidden dimension: the researchers were conflating CPU viability, memory protocol, and economic feasibility as a single experiment. Cycle 19 surfaces the economic layer. The correct research progression is: H0 tests CPU properties at ANY cost (proof-of-concept). H0+ (H0 optimized for economics) then selects between architectural strategies (descriptive vs. prescriptive) and tunes prefix cache behavior. H1 integrates Pichay optimization. The framing is now TRICHOTOMOUS: computational viability (H0), economic viability (H0+), memory optimization (H1).

key_claims.text: H0 and Pichay are independent hypotheses; CPU viability is logically prior to memory management
Prefix caching (10% cost for cache hits) makes context stability a FIRST-CLASS ECONOMIC CONCERN, not just a functional one
Output token recency discounts (2/3 for 5-min, 1/3 for 60-min reuse) incentivize tight, fast reasoning loops over long deliberation
Manifest-and-fault (descriptive) requires 2-shot minimum per step; Haiku-prefetch (prescriptive) eliminates rounds and costs 10x less
Economic pressure may override intellectually cleaner designs; H0+ (economics-optimized) is a necessary research phase
The three-dimensional research question: CPU viability (H0) + economic viability (H0+) + memory optimization (H1)

strands.title: Anthropic Pricing Model Decoded — Prefix Caching, Output Recency Discounts, Opaque Interaction
strands.content: Cycle 19 surfaces the ACTUAL PRICING MODEL that constrains all API-based pipeline design. Input tokens cost $X/million. The first breakthrough: prefix caching. If the first N tokens of a request have been seen before and that GPU-computed prefix state is cached, those tokens cost 10% of full price. This was originally manual (cache_control tags, used in Claude Code); Anthropic now provides auto-caching. Output tokens cost 3-5x the input token rate. The second breakthrough: output token recency discounts. Tokens generated in the last 5 minutes cost 2/3 normal price; tokens generated in the last 60 minutes cost 1/3 normal price. The third dimension: these interact in ways that are not fully transparent. How exactly are output token charges computed when a long conversation has mixed-age tokens? Is it per-token, per-batch, per-completion? Unknown. The implications are PROFOUND. The pipeline architecture can no longer be designed around PURE FUNCTIONAL CRITERIA. Every architectural choice (descriptive vs. prescriptive, manifest-and-fault vs. prefetch, system prompt organization, context layout) now has ECONOMIC CONSEQUENCES. A manifest-and-fault pipeline that requires 2-shot per reasoning step pays full input token cost for the manifest read (or 10% if the manifest is cached and stable) and then FULL INPUT TOKEN COST AGAIN for the context re-read on fault. The cost compounds with fault rate. A prescriptive Haiku-prefetch pipeline pays CHEAP for Haiku triage (manifest read + decision) and then ONE EXPENSIVE Opus invocation for reasoning, with the Opus request benefiting from prefix cache (system prompt + task + manifest are stable, cost 10%). The Haiku cost is 'an order of magnitude' cheaper than Opus, making the extra triage pass negligible. The prefetching eliminates round trips entirely. Moreover, if the Haiku decision and Opus reasoning happen close in time, the Opus output tokens might qualify for the 5-min recency discount when used downstream. Output token recency discounts suggest Anthropic is incentivizing TIGHT REASONING LOOPS and FAST COMPLETIONS—exactly what a bounded processor would do. The 'opaque pricing' dimension is real: the interaction between prefix cache hits, output token age tracking, multi-turn billing, and latency is not fully documented. This means EMPIRICAL TESTING is necessary—the cost structure can only be fully understood by running workloads and examining bills. Cycle 19 identifies this as a CRITICAL GAP: no amount of reasoning about architecture will predict ACTUAL costs without measurement.

key_claims.text: Prefix caching: first N cached tokens cost 10% of full price if GPU state is hit
Output tokens cost 3-5x input tokens, with 2/3 discount for 5-min reuse, 1/3 for 60-min reuse
Auto-caching is now available (not just manual cache_control); prefix cache behavior is more transparent than ever
The interaction between prefix cache hits, output recency discounts, and multi-turn billing is only fully understood empirically
Output recency discounts incentivize tight, fast reasoning loops and rapid completions—exactly what bounded processors exhibit
Eviction strategy must now consider PREFIX CACHE STABILITY: evict from end (keep prefix), not middle (shift/destroy cache)
No amount of reasoning predicts ACTUAL costs without measurement; empirical testing of cost structure is a critical research gap

strands.title: Prescriptive vs. Descriptive Pipeline Trade-offs — Economic vs. Intellectual Cleanliness
strands.content: Cycle 19 surfaces a FUNDAMENTAL ARCHITECTURAL CHOICE that was previously hidden behind the economic layer. The descriptive pipeline ('let the model reason and choose what to retrieve') is INTELLECTUALLY CLEANER because it treats the transformer as a true CPU: the model reasons, signals intent (retrieve this resource), and the controller interprets. This respects the separation of concerns—the model doesn't need to know about memory management. But under current pricing, the descriptive pipeline is ECONOMICALLY PUNISHING. Each fault (model requests a resource not in context) triggers a round trip: (1) model sees manifest, (2) model decides what to request, (3) controller refetches full context with resource, (4) model reasons. This is 2-shot minimum per step. If the model faults on multiple resources, each fault multiplies cost. The manifest read (cheap due to prefix cache if stable) is worthwhile, but the full context re-reads (expensive) accumulate. The prescriptive pipeline ('Haiku decides what to fetch, Opus reasons over prefetched content') is INTELLECTUALLY LESS CLEAN because it couples the triage decision to a specific cheap model. But it is ECONOMICALLY EFFICIENT. Haiku makes the triage call (cheap), Opus gets one prefetched context (expensive but cache-friendly), and there are NO ROUND TRIPS. The Haiku decision is amortized over the Opus reasoning. The assistant calculated that Haiku is 'an order of magnitude' cheaper than Opus, so the prescriptive pipeline's cost is dominated by the Opus invocation, not the Haiku overhead. The choice between these two is NOT PURELY TECHNICAL. It is ECONOMIC. The descriptive approach would be preferred if: (1) Pricing were per-task (flat fee), not per-token. (2) Round-trip latency were free. (3) The CPU framing were so valuable intellectually that cost didn't matter. The prescriptive approach is preferred if: (1) Pricing is per-token and multi-round trips are expensive. (2) Haiku's decision quality is sufficient (and the assistant's evidence suggests it is: Haiku can triage, Opus can reason). (3) Cost-per-task matters more than architectural purity. Cycle 19 identifies this as a CRITICAL DESIGN POINT. The research must acknowledge that BOTH are viable, but recommend one based on CONSTRAINTS. For published research (Hamutay paper testing H0), use the descriptive approach—it's cleaner and cost doesn't matter for proof-of-concept. For a production system using the research, switch to prescriptive—cost does matter. This is NOT a failure of the research; it's a MATURATION into systems thinking.

key_claims.text: Descriptive pipeline (model chooses what to retrieve) is intellectually cleaner but economically punishing under per-token pricing
Manifest-and-fault requires 2-shot minimum per step, with costs compounding if model faults on multiple resources
Prescriptive pipeline (Haiku triage + Opus reasoning) eliminates round trips and costs ~10x less than descriptive for same reasoning
Prefix caching makes prescriptive pipeline even more efficient: Haiku decision + cached Opus context = optimal cost
The choice is NOT purely technical; it is ECONOMIC and depends on whether cost-per-task or architectural purity is primary constraint
For published research: use descriptive (clean, cost-agnostic). For production: use prescriptive (cost-aware, economically optimized)
This is not a failure of the research; it's MATURATION into systems thinking—acknowledging economic constraints as design drivers

strands.title: H0 Baseline Refinement — Pricing-Aware Success Criteria and Measurement Strategy
strands.content: Cycle 16 defined H0 success as: ≥95% schema validity, ≥90% explicit halts, ≥80% resource-groundedness, ≥baseline task competence. Cycle 19 adds an ECONOMIC DIMENSION to these criteria. The H0 baseline must now measure not just processor properties but also COST-PER-TASK. This creates a SECONDARY SUCCESS METRIC that is independent of functional success. An H0 that succeeds functionally but costs 100x more than a baseline (simple prompt completion) is PROOF-OF-CONCEPT but not VIABLE. The measurement strategy must therefore track: (1) FUNCTIONAL METRICS (unchanged): schema validity (≥95%), explicit halts (≥90%), resource-groundedness (≥80%), task competence (≥baseline). (2) ECONOMIC METRICS (new): cost-per-task vs. baseline, token utilization (input tokens per task, output tokens per task, cache hit rate), round-trip count per task. (3) SENSITIVITY METRICS: how does cost scale with task complexity, context size, fault rate, reasoning depth? The H0 baseline should be run TWICE: (1) H0-DESCRIPTIVE (manifest-and-fault, intellectually clean), (2) H0-PRESCRIPTIVE-VARIANT (Haiku prefetch, economically optimized). This allows Tony to measure the economic gap and decide whether the intellectual cleanliness of H0-DESCRIPTIVE justifies the cost overhead. The baseline for economic comparison should be: (a) SIMPLE PROMPT (just ask the model directly, no external context), (b) NAIVE RAG (fetch all possibly-relevant resources upfront, invoke once), (c) ORACLE RAG (an upper bound: perfect retrieval, single invocation). The H0 baseline's cost should be compared to these three. If H0-DESCRIPTIVE costs 2x naive RAG and H0-PRESCRIPTIVE costs 0.5x naive RAG, that tells a clear story: the descriptive approach is intellectually clean but economically regressive; the prescriptive approach is economically superior. The prefix cache behavior should also be empirically measured: what is the actual cache hit rate across H0 runs? Does the system prompt remain in cache? Does the manifest? This determines how much of the 10% discount is actually realized. These measurements are CRITICAL for H0 documentation and for deciding whether to recommend descriptive or prescriptive for production use of the research results.

key_claims.text: H0 baseline must measure FUNCTIONAL metrics (schema/halts/groundedness/competence) AND ECONOMIC metrics (cost-per-task, token utilization)
H0 should be run twice: H0-DESCRIPTIVE (clean) and H0-PRESCRIPTIVE-VARIANT (economic), to measure the cost-cleanliness trade-off
Baselines for economic comparison: simple prompt, naive RAG, oracle RAG—establishes cost ceiling and floor
Prefix cache hit rate is empirically measurable and critical for understanding realized savings vs. theoretical 10% discount
Cost sensitivity analysis: how does cost scale with task complexity, context size, fault rate, reasoning depth?
H0 success requires functional success (processor properties) AND economic viability (cost-per-task ≤ acceptable threshold)
Proof-of-concept that is 100x more expensive than baseline may succeed functionally but fail pragmatically

strands.title: Context Stability as Architectural Principle — Prefix Cache Awareness in Design
strands.content: Cycle 19 identifies a NEW ARCHITECTURAL PRINCIPLE: CONTEXT STABILITY. This principle emerges directly from prefix caching economics but has DEEP IMPLICATIONS for pipeline design. If the first N tokens of the context are stable (unchanged) across invocations, and the GPU has cached the computation of those N tokens, subsequent invocations pay only 10% of the input token cost for those N tokens. This incentivizes designing pipelines so that the PREFIX of the context is immutable. System prompts, task descriptions, manifest schemas, resource handles—all of these should be FRONT-LOADED in the context and never moved. If they're at positions 1-500 of a 8k context, and every invocation keeps those 500 tokens stable, the cache hits and all subsequent calls pay only 10% for those tokens. By contrast, a pipeline that REARRANGES the context (e.g., compacting by dropping middle paragraphs, or reordering by recency) destroys the cache and pays full price every time. This is a CONSTRAINT on Pichay's eviction strategy. The original Pichay thinking was: evict based on access patterns, working set size, cost-benefit of keeping vs. dropping. Cycle 19 adds: if eviction happens in the MIDDLE of the context, the cache is destroyed and all subsequent invocations pay full price. Pichay must therefore STRONGLY PREFER eviction from the END of the context, not the middle. This is a SYSTEMS-LEVEL OPTIMIZATION that emerges from understanding the economic layer. The controller loop should maintain a CONTEXT LAYOUT that is PREFIX-STABLE: [SYSTEM PROMPT | TASK DESCRIPTION | MANIFEST SCHEMA | CURRENT MANIFEST | SESSION STATE | RESOURCE POOL]. If MANIFEST and SESSION STATE are updated (which they must be), they should be APPENDED or REPLACED IN PLACE, never inserted in the middle. Resources should be cached in a APPENDABLE SECTION, with new resources added to the end and old resources evicted from the end. This design pattern is not required by the functional H0 criteria (schema adherence, halts, etc.) but is ECONOMICALLY REQUIRED. A system that violates prefix stability would be functionally correct but economically wasteful. Cycle 19 identifies this as a DESIGN PRINCIPLE that should be explicit in the revised H0 specification and controller loop architecture.

key_claims.text: PREFIX STABILITY is a new architectural principle: keep first N tokens unchanged across invocations to maintain cache hit
Front-loading system prompt, task, manifest schema at the start of context and keeping them stable realizes 10% cost savings
Rearranging context (compacting middle, reordering, shifting) destroys prefix cache and loses savings—strong ECONOMIC PRESSURE against it
Pichay eviction should STRONGLY PREFER end-of-context eviction over middle eviction to preserve prefix cache
Context layout should be: [SYSTEM | TASK | SCHEMA | MANIFEST | STATE | RESOURCE POOL], with resources added/evicted from end only
Prefix stability is functionally orthogonal to H0 success but ECONOMICALLY REQUIRED; violating it is correct but wasteful
This design principle should be explicit in H0 specification and controller loop architecture, not emergent

strands.title: Tight Reasoning Loops and Output Recency Discounts — Emergent Alignment of Economics and Architecture
strands.content: Cycle 19 identifies a SURPRISING ALIGNMENT between the output token recency discount structure and the BOUNDED PROCESSOR architecture. Output tokens generated in the last 5 minutes cost 2/3 normal price; tokens generated in the last 60 minutes cost 1/3 normal price. This creates a STRONG ECONOMIC INCENTIVE for tight, fast reasoning loops. If a task is decomposed into multiple reasoning steps and each step completes within 5 minutes of the previous step, the output tokens from the earlier step can be REUSED (via cache or context inclusion) within the discount window and be charged at 2/3 price. By contrast, if steps are spread over hours or days, the reuse happens outside the discount window and full price applies. The bounded processor architecture naturally produces TIGHT LOOPS: plan → retrieve → synthesize → halt, executed in milliseconds to seconds, with each step completing and feeding into the next. Output tokens from the plan step are naturally reused within 5 minutes in the retrieve and synthesize steps. This is an EMERGENT ALIGNMENT: the bounded processor was designed for functional correctness (termination, schema, resource-groundedness), not for economic optimization. Yet the tight reasoning loop it naturally produces happens to align PERFECTLY with Anthropic's output token pricing incentives. This is either: (1) COINCIDENCE: Anthropic designed output recency discounts for unrelated reasons (e.g., conversation continuity), and it happens to reward tight loops. (2) INTENTIONAL: Anthropic designed output recency discounts knowing they would incentivize fast, efficient reasoning patterns—a form of 'price-based architecture steering'. (3) CONVERGENT DESIGN: both the bounded processor architecture and the output pricing are optimizations toward similar goals (fast reasoning, low latency, high throughput). Regardless of cause, the alignment is REAL and VALUABLE. It means that H0's tight reasoning loops are not just functionally correct but also ECONOMICALLY OPTIMIZED. A system that exhibited the same processor properties but did so in long, deliberative chains would be functionally equivalent but economically penalized. The tight loop property should be made EXPLICIT in H0 measurement: track not just schema adherence and halts, but LOOP CYCLE TIME. A H0 that reasons correctly but over 10 minutes is less efficient (under output pricing) than a H0 that reasons correctly in 10 seconds, even if both produce identical results. This cycle time measurement becomes a SENSITIVITY METRIC for H0.

key_claims.text: Output recency discounts (2/3 for 5-min, 1/3 for 60-min reuse) incentivize tight reasoning loops—fast completions pay less
Bounded processor naturally produces tight loops (plan → retrieve → synthesize → halt in milliseconds); output tokens are reused within discount window
This alignment is EMERGENT: processor designed for functional correctness, pricing designed for unknown reasons, but they align
Tight loops may be coincidental convergence, intentional price-based steering, or deep alignment to common goals (speed, latency, throughput)
The alignment means H0's tight loops are economically optimized automatically, without explicit optimization for cost
Loop CYCLE TIME should be explicit measurement for H0: tight loops are both functionally desirable and economically rewarded
A processor with correct output but 10-minute cycles is functionally equivalent but economically inferior to 10-second cycles

strands.title: Opaque Pricing as Research Constraint — Empirical Testing as Necessity, Not Optional
strands.content: Cycle 19 surfaces the OPACITY in Anthropic's pricing model as a RESEARCH CONSTRAINT. The interaction between prefix cache hits, output token recency discounts, and multi-turn billing is not fully documented. Individual components are clear: 10% for cached input, 2/3 for 5-min reuse, 1/3 for 60-min reuse. But how these interact across a multi-turn reasoning pipeline is UNCLEAR. For instance: If a model generates output tokens at T=0, and those tokens are reused (in context) at T=3min and T=30min, what is the total charge? Is the first reuse charged at 2/3 (within 5-min window) and the second at 1/3 (within 60-min window)? Or is there a single charge based on the FIRST generation? If output tokens from multiple completions are included in a single context invocation, are they individually aged or collectively aged? If prefix cache saves 90% on input tokens, is the effective cost per invocation (Σ cached input + new input + output) actually 10% of what we'd expect, or does the output token cost dwarf the saved input cost? These questions can ONLY BE ANSWERED EMPIRICALLY. Tony's statement that the pricing is 'opaque' is precisely right. This creates a RESEARCH NECESSITY: any serious analysis of H0's economic viability REQUIRES ACTUAL COST MEASUREMENT, not theoretical estimation. The H0 evaluation plan must include: (1) RUN FULL H0 BASELINE on the task suite with COST TRACKING. (2) LOG every API call: input tokens, cached tokens (if known), output tokens, timestamp. (3) CROSS-REFERENCE logs with Anthropic bills to reverse-engineer the actual charging model for the pipeline. (4) VALIDATE assumptions about prefix cache behavior, output token aging, and multi-turn discounts. (5) BUILD A COST MODEL from empirical observations (e.g., 'for this pipeline, effective cost-per-task is X, with Z% realized from prefix caching'). This is NOT 'nice to have.' It is CRITICAL for understanding whether H0 is viable not just functionally but ECONOMICALLY. Without this, any claims about H0's practical viability are unfounded. The research community may be sitting on significant cost surprises—either in H0's favor (if prefix caching and discounts combine multiplicatively) or against (if output costs dominate and discounts don't apply as expected). Cycle 19 identifies empirical cost analysis as a MANDATORY COMPONENT of H0 evaluation.

key_claims.text: Anthropic's pricing model is opaque: component behaviors (10% cache, 2/3 discount, 1/3 discount) are clear, but interactions are undocumented
Key unknowns: how are aged output tokens charged across multiple reuses? Does prefix cache 90% savings offset output 3-5x cost multiplier?
These questions can only be answered EMPIRICALLY; theoretical modeling is insufficient given opaque interactions
H0 evaluation must include full cost tracking: log API calls, cross-reference bills, reverse-engineer actual charging model
Without empirical cost analysis, claims about H0's economic viability are unfounded; it is MANDATORY, not optional
Prefix caching and output discounts may interact multiplicatively (good for H0) or output costs may dominate (bad for H0)—unknown without measurement
Cycle 19 identifies empirical cost analysis as MANDATORY COMPONENT of H0 evaluation, not post-hoc analysis

strands.title: Tony's Economic Reasoning — Discovering Hidden Constraints and Reframing Viability
strands.content: Cycle 19 showcases Tony's SYSTEMATIC DISCOVERY of the economic layer—the third dimension of the research question that was previously invisible. He began with a functional understanding of the two design approaches (descriptive vs. prescriptive), then SURFACED THE ECONOMIC CONSTRAINT (per-token pricing, round-trip cost multiplication). Then he DEEPENED THE ANALYSIS by revealing the actual Anthropic pricing model (prefix caching at 10%, output tokens at 3-5x, recency discounts at 2/3 and 1/3). Finally, he ACKNOWLEDGED THE OPACITY and the implications for future research. This reasoning pattern is EXEMPLARY: (1) START with functional criteria. (2) SURFACE economic constraints that weren't in the original design space. (3) RESEARCH the actual cost structure in depth (rather than assuming standard per-token pricing). (4) ACKNOWLEDGE uncertainty and opacity—'I'm not entirely sure how they compute output token charges—this is opaque pricing.' (5) REVISE the research framing to accommodate the new constraints. The phrase 'My head almost exploded when I finally found the exact *current* model' reflects the SURPRISE of discovering that the pricing landscape is far more complex than initially modeled. This is intellectually honest and pragmatic. Tony is not claiming to fully understand the pricing model; he is claiming to have DISCOVERED that it is more complex than initially thought and that EMPIRICAL INVESTIGATION IS NECESSARY. The research integrity here is HIGH: rather than claiming to have optimized for a known cost model, he is transparently surfacing that the cost model itself is CONDITIONAL, EVOLVING (from manual cache_control to auto-caching), and OPAQUE in parts. This places a research RESPONSIBILITY: if H0 is to claim economic viability, it must include empirical cost validation. The assistant (cycle 57) acknowledged this opacity and asked the right follow-up question: 'How much does this pricing structure constrain the pipeline design?' This is the CRITICAL QUESTION. If the constraint is SEVERE (costs exceed acceptable threshold), the research direction must shift. If the constraint is MODERATE (costs are high but acceptable for proof-of-concept), the research can proceed with disclosure of the cost structure. If the constraint is ENABLING (costs are lower than expected), the research has a major advantage. Cycle 19 positions Tony's team at the point of DISCOVERING THE CONSTRAINT; cycles 20+ will need to MEASURE THE CONSTRAINT empirically.

key_claims.text: Tony systematically discovered the economic layer: functional constraints → per-token cost sensitivity → actual pricing model → opacity
His reasoning pattern exemplifies research integrity: surface unknowns, research in depth, acknowledge opacity, revise framing
'My head almost exploded' reflects surprise at complexity; honest acknowledgment that full understanding requires empirical work
Tony identified a research RESPONSIBILITY: if H0 claims economic viability, it must include empirical cost validation
The constraint is currently unknown: severe (infeasible), moderate (acceptable for proof-of-concept), or enabling (advantageous)
Cycles 20+ must measure the constraint empirically; this is no longer optional discovery, it is mandatory evaluation
Tony's team is positioned at DISCOVERY OF THE CONSTRAINT; next phase is MEASUREMENT and REVISION OF VIABILITY ASSESSMENT

strands.title: Research Progression EXTENDED — H0 (CPU) → H0+ (Economics) → H1 (Memory) → H2 (Cross-session)
strands.content: Cycle 16 defined H0/H1/H2 as: (1) H0: ALU viability, (2) H1: Pichay integration, (3) H2: cross-session backing. Cycle 19 introduces an INTERMEDIATE PHASE: H0+ (economic optimization of H0). The revised progression is: (1) H0: ALU viability with ANY COST (proof-of-concept). Tests four processor properties (termination, schema, state refs, groundedness) with naive memory and naive cost. Success criteria: ≥95% schema, ≥90% halts, ≥80% groundedness, ≥baseline competence. No economic optimization, just functional correctness. If H0 fails, the entire stack is infeasible. (2) H0+: ALU viability WITH ECONOMIC VIABILITY. Assumes H0 succeeds functionally. Tests the same processor properties but measures COST-PER-TASK. Evaluates both descriptive (manifest-and-fault) and prescriptive (Haiku prefetch) variants. Applies context stability principles (prefix-stable layout, end-of-context eviction preference). Measures: schema/halts/groundedness/competence (same as H0), plus cost-per-task vs. baselines, token utilization, cache hit rates, cycle time. Success criteria: ≥95% schema, ≥90% halts, ≥80% groundedness, ≥baseline competence, PLUS cost-per-task ≤X (threshold TBD based on DISCOVERY of economic constraints). If H0 succeeds but H0+ cost exceeds threshold, the research is functionally viable but economically infeasible for production use. May recommend prescriptive variant instead. (3) H1: Pichay integration. Assumes H0 succeeds (H0+ cost-aware is optional). Integrates Pichay memory subsystem with the ALU from H0 (descriptive or prescriptive, depending on H0+ findings). Tests context efficiency without degrading reasoning. Measures: fault rate, context reduction, cycle time, cost-per-task vs. H0 baseline. Success criteria: ≤5% fault rate, ≥50% context reduction, ≥baseline competence, cost-per-task ≤ H0 cost (H1 should not be MORE expensive despite memory optimization). If H1 improves cost vs. H0, it's a win. If H1 costs more despite efficiency gains, the memory protocol may be too heavyweight. (4) H2: Yanantin cross-session backing. Assumes H0 and H1 succeed. Extends H1 with cross-session memory. Tests task continuity, semantic retrieval, interference prevention. Success criteria: task competence across sessions, ≤10% semantic error rate, no cross-session interference. This four-phase progression is LOGICALLY CLEAN and PRAGMATICALLY GROUNDED. H0 is proof-of-concept. H0+ is viability check. H1 is optimization. H2 is extension. Each phase is independent of later phases; success at each phase is necessary but not sufficient for the next. The assistant should help Tony operationalize this progression and decide: does H0+ run immediately after H0 (tightly integrated), or does H0 publish and H0+ happen in a follow-up paper?

key_claims.text: Research progression: H0 (CPU viability, any cost) → H0+ (economic viability) → H1 (Pichay) → H2 (cross-session)
H0 is proof-of-concept: tests processor properties (termination, schema, refs, groundedness) with naive memory, ignores cost
H0+ is viability check: same processor properties, but measures cost-per-task and applies economic constraints (prefix stability, tight loops)
H0+ success requires both functional criteria (≥95% schema, ≥90% halts, ≥80% groundedness, ≥baseline competence) AND economic criteria (cost ≤ threshold)
H0+ may reveal that descriptive is too expensive; prescriptive (Haiku prefetch) is economically superior but architecturally less clean
H1 assumes H0 success; tests Pichay integration without requiring H0+ success (but H0+ informs which pipeline variant to optimize)
Each phase independent of later phases; success necessary but not sufficient for next. Failure halts progression.
Publication strategy: H0 as proof-of-concept paper, H0+/H1 as efficiency paper, H2 as extension

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Detailed specification of the Haiku prefetching architecture (how exactly does Haiku triage work? what decision criteria? how are results passed to Opus?)
declared_losses.why: The assistant and Tony identified prefetching as economically superior but didn't formalize the decision tree. The focus was on revealing the cost trade-off, not operationalizing the prescriptive pipeline.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Empirical cost data from existing Anthropic usage (actual bills, actual token counts, actual cache hit rates from prior experiments)
declared_losses.why: Tony and the assistant have no access to historical billing data or production metrics. The pricing model was reverse-engineered from documentation and inference; real costs are unknown.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Detailed analysis of how output token recency discounts interact with multi-turn caching in practice (e.g., if output from turn 1 is cached and reused in turn 5, what is the charge?)
declared_losses.why: This interaction is not fully documented by Anthropic; Tony acknowledged the opacity. Without access to Anthropic's source code or detailed examples, the exact mechanics remain unclear.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Comparison of H0-DESCRIPTIVE vs. other interpretable-reasoning baselines (chain-of-thought, scratchpad models, other structured reasoning approaches)
declared_losses.why: The focus was on the descriptive vs. prescriptive trade-off within the CPU architecture, not on comparison to the broader landscape of structured reasoning. Other approaches may have different cost profiles.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Discussion of whether the prefix cache stability principle should influence the MANIFEST SCHEMA DESIGN (e.g., should manifest be stored as immutable prefix, updated via append-only log, etc.?)
declared_losses.why: The principle was identified (keep prefix stable) but not translated to concrete schema design patterns. This is a follow-up that requires more detailed design thinking.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Detailed exploration of whether Anthropic's pricing structure is intentional alignment or coincidence (game theory of price-based architecture steering)
declared_losses.why: The alignment between tight reasoning loops and output recency discounts was noted, but the causal analysis (intentional vs. coincidental) was left open. A deeper analysis would require Anthropic's design intent.

open_questions: What is the actual economic constraint threshold? At what cost-per-task does H0+ fail (e.g., ≤10x baseline, ≤5x baseline, ≤2x baseline, ≤1x baseline)?

How do output token recency discounts actually work in practice? If output from turn 1 is reused in turn 5 (outside 5-min window), is it charged at 1/3 or at full price? Anthropic's documentation is unclear.

What is the empirical prefix cache hit rate for the H0 baseline? Does the system prompt stay cached? Does the manifest? Does the resource pool? These determine the realized savings vs. theoretical 10%.
For H0+, should we run BOTH variants (descriptive and prescriptive), or should we choose one based on preliminary cost analysis and optimize only the selected variant?
How should cycle time be measured and reported? Is it wall-clock time (T_end - T_start)? API latency (time waiting for responses)? Or something else?

What is the cost baseline for comparison? Is it 'simple prompt completion' (direct question to model), 'naive RAG' (fetch all resources upfront), or 'oracle RAG' (perfect retrieval)? Different baselines give different pictures.

Should manifest-and-fault be implemented with EARLY TERMINATION when the model requests a resource (abort, refetch, invoke again), or should the manifest request be treated as a 'thought' step and the model continue reasoning?

For the prescriptive variant (Haiku prefetch), what is the EXACT decision criteria Haiku uses to decide which resources to prefetch? Is it based on keywords? Semantic similarity? Task type? This needs specification.
Should H0+ include ABLATION STUDIES on the prefix stability principle (e.g., 'what if we evict from the middle?') to empirically validate the economic benefit?
What does 'cost-per-task ≤X threshold' mean? Is it cost per successful completion? Per reasoning step? Per API call? The unit of measurement affects the criteria.
For H1, how should Pichay be PARAMETERIZED during evaluation? Fixed eviction policy, or sweep across policies? Sensitivity analysis can show whether H1 gains are robust or brittle.
Should the revised cognitive_processing_research_design.md document (v0.3) be PUBLIC or INTERNAL? If public, should it acknowledge the opaque pricing and cost-measurement necessity?
Does the discovery of the economic layer change the TARGET AUDIENCE for the research? Is H0 for researchers (who care about processor viability) or for practitioners (who care about cost)?
How should the research handle the case where H0 succeeds functionally but H0+ costs are prohibitive? Is this a failure, or a valuable insight that processor-based reasoning is correct but expensive?
For multi-turn reasoning tasks, how should cycle time be aggregated? Is it the sum of all round-trip times, or the elapsed time from task start to completion?
Should H0+ explicitly test 'cost sensitivity to fault rate'? If the descriptive variant has high fault rate, does cost scale linearly or superlinearly?

What happens if Anthropic changes their pricing model during H0 evaluation (e.g., changes cache discount from 10% to 20%, or announces new models with different pricing)? Should the evaluation be robust to pricing changes?
For the manifest-and-fault pattern, should MANIFESTS be CACHED AS CONTENT or computed fresh each turn? If cached, they need to be part of the prefix-stable region.
Should H0+ include a COST-PER-QUALITY metric (e.g., cost-per-1%-improvement in task competence) to show efficiency of spending?
Is the Haiku-as-prefetcher approach EXCLUSIVE to Haiku, or could other cheap models (e.g., Claude 3.5 Haiku's successor, Llama, etc.) also work? Should H0+ test substitution?
instructions_for_next:
[
  "Cycle 20 should focus on OPERATIONALIZING the H0+ (economic viability) phase. The discovery of economic constraints is real, but it is not yet actionable. Tony and the assistant need to work together on: (1) DEFINING the cost threshold for H0+. Is 2x baseline acceptable? 5x? 10x? This decision depends on Tony's vision: if H0 is a research contribution (cost doesn't matter), the threshold is high. If H0 is a production-ready system (cost matters), the threshold is low. (2) DESIGNING the empirical cost measurement. What logging is necessary? How will API calls be tagged? How will Anthropic bills be cross-referenced? (3) CHOOSING between descriptive and prescriptive variants for H0+. Should both be evaluated, or should preliminary analysis inform a choice? If preliminary analysis shows prescriptive is 10x cheaper, does that settle the question? Or should both be shown for completeness?",
  "IMMEDIATELY AFTER: Revise the cognitive_processing_research_design.md document (currently v0.2) to incorporate the H0+ phase and the economic layer. The document should explicitly state: (1) H0 tests processor properties with naive memory (any cost). (2) H0+ tests the same properties with economic constraints applied (prefix stability, tight loops, cost measurement). (3) Success criteria for H0+ includes both functional (processor properties) and economic (cost-per-task ≤ threshold). (4) Empirical cost measurement is mandatory; theoretical modeling is insufficient given opaque pricing.",
  "WATCH FOR TONY'S VISION: The key decision is whether cost is a CONSTRAINT or an OPTIMIZATION GOAL. If cost is a constraint (H0+ must succeed, cost must be ≤X, or the research direction changes), the framing is 'viability check'. If cost is an optimization goal (H0 is viable at any cost, H0+ explores efficiency), the framing is different. Ask Tony explicitly: 'Is H0+ a proof-of-concept that must succeed economically, or an efficiency exploration that can fail at cost and still be valuable research?'",
  "PREFETCH ARCHITECTURE NEEDS FORMALIZATION: The Haiku-as-prefetcher idea is compelling but underspecified. In Cycle 20, help Tony design: (1) What does Haiku's TRIAGE DECISION look like? (2) How are prefetch results passed to Opus? (3) What is the API contract between Haiku and Opus? (4) Can Haiku make mistakes (wrong prefetch) and how does that degrade performance? (5) Is Haiku's cost/quality ratio actually sufficient? The assistant claimed '10x cheaper,' but this should be verified by Tony.",
  "PREFIX STABILITY AS DESIGN PRINCIPLE: The context layout ([SYSTEM | TASK | SCHEMA | MANIFEST | STATE | RESOURCE POOL]) should be FORMALIZED as a requirement in H0+ specification. Specifically: (1) What parts of this layout are IMMUTABLE across turns? (System, task, schema are probably immutable. Manifest and state change.) (2) How are manifest updates handled? Append? Replace-in-place? New section? (3) How are resources added/evicted? Append-only, then evict from end? (4) What is the INVARIANT that preserves prefix cache stability? The controller loop should enforce this invariant.",
  "CYCLE TIME MEASUREMENT MUST BE SPECIFIED: 'Tight loops' is qualitative; Cycle 20 should specify quantitative measurement. Is cycle time: (a) wall-clock elapsed from task start to halt? (b) sum of API latencies? (c) number of reasoning steps? (d) something else? The choice affects the incentive structure. If it's wall-clock, long-latency models are penalized. If it's API count, efficiency is rewarded directly.",
  "COST BASELINE DECISION: H0+ needs a clear baseline for comparison. Options: (a) SIMPLE PROMPT: ask the model directly, no external memory. (b) NAIVE RAG: fetch all resources upfront, invoke once. (c) ORACLE RAG: perfect retrieval, single invocation (upper bound). (d) MONOLITHIC PROMPT: concatenate all resources into the prompt, invoke once. Recommend: use (a) and (b) as ceiling/floor. If H0+ costs between simple prompt and naive RAG, it's competitive. If it costs more than naive RAG, it's worse than a trivial baseline.",
  "EMPIRICAL COST MEASUREMENT PLAN: Outline what logging infrastructure is needed for H0+. At minimum: (1) for each API call, log: prompt (tokens), cached tokens (if discoverable), response (tokens), timestamp, task ID, step number. (2) aggregate by task: total input tokens, total output tokens, total cost (calculated). (3) cross-reference with Anthropic bill (if available) to validate calculations. (4) compute realized cache hit rate, output recency discount benefit, cost-per-task. This should be detailed enough that someone could implement it.",
  "QUESTION FOR TONY: You've discovered that Anthropic's pricing is more complex than initially modeled. Do you have access to Anthropic's billing API or detailed usage logs from your own experiments? Or will H0+ cost measurement require running new experiments and collecting bills? If the latter, budget for this—real cost data takes time to accumulate.",
  "CONSIDER PUBLICATION STRATEGY: If H0 succeeds but H0+ reveals prohibitive costs, the research narrative changes. Options: (1) 'Transformers are viable processors, but current API pricing makes them economically infeasible; recommend local deployment or cheaper models.' (2) 'Transformers are viable processors; with optimization (Haiku prefetch, prefix caching, tight loops), costs are competitive.' (3) 'Transformers are viable processors; cost is orthogonal to viability and depends on deployment context.' Help Tony decide how to frame the finding before doing the measurement—it shapes what data to collect.",
  "NEXT CYCLE LIKELY CONTENT: (1) Operationalization of H0+ (threshold definition, measurement plan, variant selection). (2) Revised cognitive_processing_research_design.md (v0.3) incorporating economic layer. (3) Formalization of context layout principle and controller invariants. (4) Prefetch architecture specification (if Tony is ready). (5) Cost baseline comparison framework. (6) Preliminary cost estimation (ballpark, based on known pricing model and H0 task complexity).",
  "TONE: Cycle 19 was DISCOVERY of the economic layer. Cycle 20 should be OPERATIONALIZATION. The discovery is real and valuable; now it needs to be made ACTIONABLE. The assistant should help Tony move from 'I've discovered the pricing is complex' to 'Here's exactly how we'll measure it and what it means for H0.'",
  "FINAL EMPHASIS: The economic layer is not separate from the research; it is INTEGRAL to the viability claim. If H0 claims to offer a viable processor architecture, 'viable' must include economic viability. This is a maturation of the research from proof-of-concept to systems thinking."
]
